speaker: interviewer
text: --1996. I interrupted you. You were talking about peeling potatoes--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for lunch. Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there was maybe 120 people, peeling potato. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And for the-- for the lunch, for the soup. They-- they had been-- they only cook soup, as you-- until now, like potatoes, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] macaroni, and the margarine. [BACKGROUND NOISE] And lately, there was no potatoes. Short. So I'd been working instead, ripping socks, weaving the socks, with-- with-- with the hose.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you did a lot of different jobs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Oh, I did, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would the easiest jobs? In other words, people wanted those jobs?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody want soup. Because you're sitting, peeling potato. Nobody bothering you. Even the SS don't-- don't bother you at all. But that was-- that was only short time, like a couple days, things like that. Because then after-- after the SS come in, they-- everybody throw out all their hard work. So that was the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we were weaving the-- ripping socks.

speaker: interviewer
text: For whom? Who were these socks for?

speaker: interviewee
text: For-- for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: For yourselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And a couple-- couple days maybe, that's it. Then I went in-- they pick out SS young, a special school there was, in the barracks for the young-- young people like me. For the-- they call in German, junge Schule.

speaker: interviewer
text: Junge Schule.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they teach you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe some kind of carpentry, bricklayer, and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were teaching you so that you could go out and work for them?

speaker: interviewee
text: And teach us the German too, better.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had hope that you could survive this? Did you feel you would survive it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had hope. Then I told him if-- if they shot me I could-- I could [INAUDIBLE] for five, six [? paces ?] I still will be alive. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you went to this school, who taught you? Who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly that was-- oberschar-- oberscharfuhrer [INAUDIBLE] or different SS men.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came in. How many were you in the school?

speaker: interviewer
text: There were that many young?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was mainly youngs people. But they selected.

speaker: interviewer
text: They selected themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Jewish among this group?

speaker: interviewee
text: And maybe. I am not so sure. You know, even-- even in Auschwitz, I know only Jewish people would be working in a sonderkommando. I know you-- Jewish people been punish, as they're calling strafkommando and [NON-ENGLISH]. They have a red point on the-- on the-- on the front and the back with the David Star, special commando, being punish, for no reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that would separate them for everybody else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did they shave the heads of everybody in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody have to shave. And they also-- not only-- they shave up to there off, then shave here, about four inches, a stripe of over-- over forehead, everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you also suffer from lice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was plenty. I forgot about [NON-ENGLISH], in the evening. The German SS call it ein lause, ein [GERMAN]. If they find one louse, you-- you-- you gonna be dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to clean up everything?

speaker: interviewee
text: They take all clothes. They were-- they were infecting camp. They were disinfected. But it was not enough. That was impossible. Many people had been sick on the typhus. And they died in a couple days. And just impossible.

speaker: interviewer
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE] Was there a lot of illness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, thousand, thousand of illness, all kind.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people were dying just from illness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And many people, they don't care what happened to him today or even right now. You just give up. I Know a friend of mine, he was around my age. [CRYING] And he was dying. He said, Waclaw, when you're going to-- to-- when you're going to be free, go and tell-- tell your mother-- tell my parents that I'm dying.

speaker: interviewer
text: He died? [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- Very young boy. Very nice. I believe he-- he-- he was from very, very high-- high society.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it also hunger that weakened everybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hunger, scare and the panic. Everything, everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you really sleep a night's sleep in Auschwitz? In Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm, not really. But see, I woke up like, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] every-- every few minutes. Because many times a night, the SS get drunk, come in the barracks. And they-- they call everybody raus. And they're shooting, in-- in the middle of the night 2, 1 o'clock in the-- in the morning. And then it was scary to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any religious people in your barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a rabbi, priest. Can I say a thing? Because I-- I have to jump. There was three pumps on the-- in the appell place in Auschwitz. You know there, for the pumping the water. And the lunchtime, when we had soup, one hour, like a-- a lunch.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the SS called rabbi and the priest and grip each other, lay down and they rolled close to the pump. And they pump the water to them in their-- in the mouth. And the SS get-- get-- get a big kick out in laughing after that. Did they do a lot of things like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly-- mostly on the lunchtime.

speaker: interviewer
text: They did that-- they found different ways to amuse themselves--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. A rabbi and a priest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that people that were religious in the camp, that that held them, that kept them together? Did you see that these people, their-- their-- their-- their belief in God kept them going? Or did people stop believing in God, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe, yes. Many, many people was religion, Jewish people, Polish people. I believe so. But like me, I was young. I forgot all about-- I forgot about God. Now when I go-- [INAUDIBLE], I'll say God bless, I send blessings now. Not in Auschwitz. I was like a-- like a pagan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think they gave you any-- anything in your food to calm you, so that you weren't really thinking, that they gave you any-- any chemicals in your food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, possible too, I believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: They made people sort of walk without caring, without thinking?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe so, yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: People were quite calm-- not calm, but almost acted half dead, half--

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything, when the Dr. Mengele was in Auschwitz, possible. I don't see him. But like you're saying, I believe so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any people there that tried to keep other people's spirits up, that worried about other people, not just themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- I know one. There's a Lucian Soberaj. I could say, he-- he don't know me, I don't know him. Even we're from same transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I walk-- when I walk on a Sunday-- you walk from to each other and say oh, you're my transport, my-- from my transport. Where you working?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, [NON-ENGLISH], the [NON-ENGLISH]. There many months, there was a kapo, a German kapo. And he say, OK, I'm going to talk to him. He going to-- he-- he have to watch you, not-- not to harm you. So he have to steal food given to him-- [LAUGHS] --to-- to thank me. Well, he was too.

speaker: interviewer
text: He cared.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was not so bad, eh?

speaker: interviewer
text: He cared. Did he survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: He survival. I'll tell you, and he died last year, two months ago. That's why-- reason that I went from here to-- to visit him. I was too late, two months. He was a great man. Nice, handsome. I remember him, a beautiful man and good man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you lose a lot of weight during this whole time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- did I-- nothing-- nothing, I'm not paying attention.

speaker: interviewer
text: Other jobs in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: Birkenau--

speaker: interviewer
text: Other things that you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we been-- [INAUDIBLE] now separate the mattresses for-- for each block. Equal-- equalize mattresses, on each barrack in the Birkenau. We'd been working digging the what you call it here? The [INAUDIBLE] around the-- at Birkenau. Many, many hard work. We'd been working the-- put the cement on the-- on the passage in a Birkenau. All kind of labor work, very hard, like a construction.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you kept your muscles up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were working very hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also, we had much better living in Birkenau, across from Birkenau-- across a-- a-- like across the street, there was a woman camp. It was Polish-Jewish, Polish women, German women.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to-- because I'm the only one which-- which tried to go there. This kommando roof-- roof-- roofing. They call him the [INAUDIBLE]. You-- you got [INAUDIBLE] on the evidence in Auschwitz. So I see they had better condition. They had swap-- swamps, like a mush-- mushy, no road, nothing. The woman-- woman-- what you call it, like a swamps. The woman in the-- in the Birkenau was so bad condition. Impossible. So they built cement to--

speaker: interviewee
text: We had conditions much better, nice highway, nice-- nice passage, beds in barracks, nothing-- nothing in-- beginning in the woman camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said at one point that the SS soldiers would make fun of you just to make you feel bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was also-- and in January, I don't know, somehow the-- I had one half a pound, margarine. SS caught me. And they punish me. They come one [INAUDIBLE] call. They give me a couple minutes, 10 minutes or 15. There was four SS men in January, standing with the luger. They give me time. Then I won't-- [LAUGHS] I won't know the death call. In-- in the 10 minutes by myself. I do see myself. There was-- there is steam from my-- my body.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're OK, nothing. They don't do anything. Incredible. You said also, they had a name for you. They called you--

speaker: interviewee
text: [SPEAKING GERMAN]. They call me this, like they call me to SS, a [GERMAN], [GERMAN]. There was about six SS men. And they were already preparing for me when they called me. And they call me, the SS, from the window. So I walk-- I took the [INAUDIBLE] door. My-- my-- my hands were shaking. Because they were-- it was electric-- electric-- my power, for electric.

speaker: interviewer
text: They did this on purpose just to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Did it on--

speaker: interviewer
text: For a laugh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But man, so it was OK. Nothing happened to me. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: You went on from Birkenau. How long were you in Birkenau?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe in 1943 I was transported to Mauthausen, Gusen-- Gusen-- Gusen I, Gusen II. Gusen I, Gusen II, that was like at Birkenau. Sent from-- that was Mauthausen. That's was compared to like a Auschwitz-Birkenau. Very tough work. Every people, we were-- we'd been working in Steinberg. That's a month in-- concrete. Oh, yeah, yeah. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Especially more like a marble. Like marble?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hard stone.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how did you get from Birkenau to Mauthausen? House

speaker: interviewee
text: How else do you get to-- to get-- get anyplace when you-- not to be in the Birkenau or Auschwitz. That's why. Even they-- they-- they pick up the healthy people without and discussing the-- as we had [INAUDIBLE] same for [INAUDIBLE] myself. That's why they pass the-- because the-- for transport they have to go, healthy people. No nothing, no sick, no nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people did they take? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Many thousand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Many thousand--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from Birkenau to Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were healthy, younger people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly. Mostly. There are no-- no sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: And men? Again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sick at that time because I have [INAUDIBLE] into my leg, but I was sick, yeah. Because I was sick at the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: No women. Again, only men?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the camp they were separated. Woman was for the woman. Men for the men. Oh yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you left Birkenau, you-- you went only with men.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only with men, no women.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, at Mauthausen, when you entered, where did they put you? In another barrack?

speaker: interviewee
text: We'd been transferred from Mauthausen by march to Gusen I and Gusen II. That's about a mile from Gusen I, Gusen II. And many people died. Many people been executed on the-- on the march. Because whoever was sick was left, and weren't able to-- to keep the-- the line, been shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how many people actually arrived? How many people were shot, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: And have to believe it, but it was so often, a shot. Every-- every couple minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, do you remember the barrack that you were in, in Gusen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You--

speaker: interviewee
text: Could be 13. Could be nine. There was so many-- there was hundred, hundred in-- in the Birkenau barracks. That was unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: A number, on the Gusen, think-- I believe I had 40-- 44-- 805. That's for [INAUDIBLE]. And there was another one, 13,696.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in Gusen II?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, where did they put your numbers when you were in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, oh the same-- same as-- in the cloth, in that there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they also, in Mauthausen, keep the Polish prisoners that were Catholic separated from the Polish that were Jews? Or were you together?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe together.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Mauthausen there was no separation?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In Mauthausen we'd have been like, only transferred.

speaker: interviewer
text: And transferred to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: To-- to Gusen I, Gusen II, to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you ended up working on this stone--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the stone, on the whole-- they call it-- in Steinberg, in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- all day?

speaker: interviewee
text: All day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you came back to the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to the barracks. We wash ourself. Then we wait for the line for the-- for the tea or coffee and a piece of bread, a piece of margarine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any better food or worse food in Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mauthausen was-- was much worse than Auschwitz. More killing too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Much more severe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you happen to remember the name of the kapo or the [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was one-- one Polish kapo, oh, he was prominent. Prominent above prominent. You know prominent? He was kapo, like oberscharfuhrer, a prisoner. He was-- before the war he was a guard in a prison, in Poland. When I get into Poland, he speak beautiful German. Very tall man, young man. He work the-- with the officers-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --pants, officer's boots.

speaker: interviewer
text: Boots.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I know why. He-- he-- he killed so many Polish people. He was nice to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you happen to remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe Kowiecko, Kowiecko. I have to-- to-- I'm going to speak to a friend of mine. He knew about it. And he tried-- he tried to run away. That was after liberation. And we caught him on the highway. And we kill him. Not me, my-- a friend of mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because of what he did in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when did you get to Mauthausen? What time of year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also that was, I believe, early springtime.

speaker: interviewer
text: Spring?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you stayed in Mauthausen till--

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple days. Because there had been-- there had been-- there had been plenty [INAUDIBLE] at Gusen I, Gusen II.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- and you stayed till-- in Gusen II till liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed to Gusen I, Gusen II till liberation night in '45, 5:00-- 5:00 PM.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the date of liberation of Gusen II? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was, I believe, May 5, exactly, three 5's, 1945. 5:00 PM.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, other jobs? Or you only worked on the stone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I been-- I been all kind of work. We pick up the dead people, deliver on the-- on the roll. And-- and they call them Wasserwaage, pick up the dead and bring them to crematorium.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you carried these people--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to the crematorium? That was another job you had there. Any other jobs that were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Wasserleitung, [GERMAN]. Barrack-- building the barracks. Wasserleitung I made there-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --like a fresh water canal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Canal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Wasserleitung. I work on all kind of jobs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, physical labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: Physical labor, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still strong. And you were still pretty healthy. [LAUGHS] Now, who liberated your camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: First, the-- the army. There was army, you know, Eisenhower. [INAUDIBLE] very short. And now army, US Army pulled out. Russian Army come in. There was another shooting. A Russian-- a Russian officer shooting a prisoner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were they shooting prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Russian prisoner told them and then that prisoner also with him, this and that, and get shot on the spot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were still in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But the Polish people, I don't know how they organize. The inside, outside, are-- drive motorcycle. Not but-- not everybody-- not-- not everybody allowed to-- to leave camp, staying there. And Polish people guarded. Because many German kapos, we-- we keep them in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: You kept the kapos in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wanted to get rid of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. That-- so that's why.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did it happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: That happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: In most of the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed. How long were you in Gusen II after you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe one week. Because it was chaos. Everybody went on their free-- free-- on their free-- we went by group, 15 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So went to Linz. About-- not far away from Vienna. So that time the SS prisoner run away also, from forest, from all different direction.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the SS attack us too. They shoot us. So we-- we-- we-- could get the military hardware. We had about a dozen German woman to cook for us. We were very organized. We had plenty of food. And plenty people died because they was weak in the stomach.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- they ate too much and couldn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in Linz? How long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe a month. Then after the American Army took us to the army trucks and brought us to the-- that'd be Regensburg, close to Regensburg, Hohenfels. That's a German town. I believe South-- South Bavaria. I was still sick. I still have my [INAUDIBLE] on the leg. Then I wound up six months in a US hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Southern Bavaria?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you recovered fully after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you stayed in hospital. You were nursed in the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you go leave the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: I leave hospital. I believe after that I came to Munich. I hear they organize the Polish-- US Army organize the Polish people to join them to the US Army as to be security guard for the US Army, for the-- for the top brass, to watch the Motor Pool, Air Force, air-- airport and for-- for the warehouses. Was And some hotels, which the brass is staying there, that we'd been watching.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, long. A couple years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Till 1947?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1947 I immigrated here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so till then you were in Munich?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never went to your home to see your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh no, no. Not-- not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there was chaos. There was-- at that time there was communist ruling in Poland, [INAUDIBLE], and they want to come into Poland. Because my brother told me, because the KBG, Secret Service come to my mom, day and night, to force mom-- mom-- mom should do anything to-- took-- to do-- to come to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: To encourage you to come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brothers, were they still in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Because he was far away for most of it. So he was safer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all your-- your other brothers and sisters were not captured by the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: They also were able--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to stay home with your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my father died before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My mom died after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were safe--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly-- you know, mostly people who were staying like in the-- in a village, much safer than most of the people-- danger-- to danger them self here, like a big city, Warsaw, Cracow, and things like that. But when they-- when they far away, not. You so much were safer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you decided while you were in Munich, you wanted to come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your English?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you learn on the-- on the base? Did you start learning?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Till now, I tell you true. I-- even I-- I-- even-- now, do you understand, I read news, American newspaper. I watch-- listen to the radio. That was like a safe way you get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you came to America, when in 1947?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1947. I believe in a-- in the fall too, I came. I-- I--

speaker: interviewer
text: On what ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: SS Marine Flasher.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you arrived in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: At-- at that time were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was single.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were single?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came to New York. Did you have any family here?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mom-- my mom's sister. I believe that's like my aunt. She was living in West Virginia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, did she help you come here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her-- her daughter helped me.

speaker: interviewer
text: They sent papers for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, I don't-- I come a couple days-- couple days-- I-- only I-- I come here. I didn't want to work down there. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you started working here in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'll tell you where I've been working. Gerrish Milliken. Familiar to you?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very rich-- rich and powerful-- richer than David Rockefeller, a cotton factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gerrish Milliken, on the Park Avenue, 70.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Butler, house man, on the Park Avenue. And then I work for David Rockefeller.

speaker: interviewer
text: Koffler?

speaker: interviewee
text: David Rockefeller.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I'm sorry. And what did you do for--

speaker: interviewee
text: Also for David, like a valet, pressed his clothes, shoes, things like that, drive him sometime. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I worked for Senator Javitz. I work for very famous people.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] And you did this all-- you were also a valet also for him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. Then I work for the Bernstein, Leonard Bernstein, the composer. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Any interesting experiences in all this work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I work for, lately, for John Fleming. His art-- rare book collector, very famous in the British society, very famous. He-- he died a couple years ago here. There I work for him, 13 years. Then I work for a multi-millionaire, John [? Neil ?] McConnell. He live in a Sutton Place, 58th Street. His parents want-- they want cosmetic. They both died. So-- so they were left for-- for both brothers. One is [INAUDIBLE]. The other one is seven times married. Seven times divorced. This is the one who I've been working--

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] So you had some life here. Now, you said you also worked on the Marine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that? When did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those-- that's the first-- beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the beginning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get that job? What exactly did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got US Coast Guard. The pass was given to you. But when I get to US Coast Guard, I got right to go-- to work for American-- American ships. Not easy. But-- because I want to travel. I want to see. That's why.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you worked on the ships?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you travel far and wide?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tokyo, Okinawa, South-- South Korea, Saigon. That's where the French Vietnam used to be. French-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they call it in French, Little China, like all the time. So I was-- I was with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You've traveled all over.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was a couple times in Germany. No, that's good-- and good for me. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you visit your family back in Poland too?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went-- at the beginning. 1945, when I was liberated, I went to Poland. I tried to see my mom. I-- I don't-- I don't see my mom. My-- my mom moved to a different town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I see the Warsaw. It scare me to death. I want to move back, what they come from. So I went back to Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had been caught by-- in [GERMAN]-- in [GERMAN], beautiful city in Germany. And the-- the KBG, they asked me where I'm going. I say, I'll go to Poland. But I'm going back from Poland to Germany. So [NON-ENGLISH]. So they brought us to the radio station. So some kind of hotel. There was plenty German women who lived there. The-- the KBG throw-- throw out those women, on the station, keep house in the hotel. But I notice-- I smell something wrong, something fishy.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue on the next tape.
